back_translation: Paul gazed intently at the Sanhedrin council and said, "Honored brothers, I have lived my life before God with a good and pure conscience until this day."
Then the high priest Ananias commanded those who were standing near Paul to slap him on the mouth.

Then Paul said to him, "God will slap you in return, you who are a whitewashed wall! You sit in judgment of me according to the Torah, but you yourself command someone to slap me — which is a violation of the Torah, isn't it?"
Those standing nearby said, "How dare you insult the high priest of God?"
Paul replied, "Brothers, I did not know that he was the high priest, for it is written, 'You shall not speak evil of a ruler of your people.'"

When Paul knew that one part of the council were Sadducees and the other part were Pharisees, he shouted in the Sanhedrin council, "Honored brothers, I am a Pharisee, a son of Pharisees. I am being put on trial because of the hope of the resurrection from the dead."
When he had said this, a dispute broke out between the Pharisees and the Sadducees, and the assembly was split into two sides.
For the Sadducees say there is no resurrection from the dead, no angels, and no spirits, but the Pharisees acknowledge both these things.

A great clamor arose, and some scribes from the party of the Pharisees stood up and contended vigorously, saying, "We find no fault in this man at all. What if a spirit or an angel has spoken to him?"

When the dispute grew more violent, the chiliarch was afraid that Paul would be torn to pieces by them, so he ordered the troop of soldiers to go down and forcibly drag him out from among them and bring him back into the barracks.
On the night of the next day, the Lord stood near Paul and said, "Take courage! For just as you have testified about me in Jerusalem, so also you must testify in Rome."
When daybreak came, certain Jews banded together and bound themselves with a curse-oath, saying that they would not eat or drink until they had killed Paul.
Those who joined in this conspiracy were more than forty men.
They went to the chief priests and the elders and said, "We have bound ourselves with a heavy curse-oath that we will not taste any food at all until we have killed Paul.

Now therefore, please petition the chiliarch together with the Sanhedrin council to bring Paul down to you, claiming that you will examine his case more thoroughly. As for us, we are ready to kill him before he reaches you."
But the son of Paul's sister heard about the ambush, so he came and entered the barracks and reported it to Paul.
Paul then called one of the centurions and said, "Please take this young man to the chiliarch, for he has something to tell him."
The centurion took the young man to the chiliarch and said, "Paul the prisoner called me and asked me to bring this young man to you, for he has something to say to you."
The chiliarch took the young man by the hand and led him to a private place, and asked, "What do you have to tell me?"
The young man said, "Certain Jews have agreed to ask you to bring Paul down to the Sanhedrin tomorrow, claiming they will examine his case more thoroughly.

Therefore do not let them persuade you, because more than forty men from among them are lying in ambush for Paul. They have bound themselves with a curse-oath that they will not eat or drink until they have killed him. Now they are ready, waiting only for your consent."
So the chiliarch released the young man, charging him, "Do not tell anyone that you have reported this to me."

The chiliarch then called two centurions and said, "Prepare two hundred foot-soldiers, seventy horsemen, and another two hundred spearmen to travel to Caesarea at about nine o'clock tonight (the third hour of the night).
Also have mounts prepared for Paul to ride, in order to bring him safely to the governor Felix."
The chiliarch also wrote a letter with the following content:
"I, Claudius Lysias, send respectful greetings to the most honorable governor Felix.
This man was seized by the Jews, and they were about to kill him, so I led the troops there and rescued him, since I had learned that he is a Roman citizen.
Because I wanted to know the cause for which they were accusing him, I brought him down to their Sanhedrin council.
I found that he was being accused over disputes about their Torah, but there was no charge worthy of the death penalty or of imprisonment.
When I was informed that there would be a plot against this man, I sent him to you immediately, and I also instructed the accusers to come and present their case before you."
So the soldiers carried out the orders, taking Paul and traveling by night to the city of Antipatris.
On the next day, the foot-soldiers let the horsemen continue the journey with Paul, and they themselves returned to the barracks.
When the horsemen entered the city of Caesarea, they delivered the letter to the governor and presented Paul to him as well.
When the governor read the letter, he asked from what province Paul was, and when he learned that he was from the province of Cilicia,
the governor said, "I will give your case a full hearing when your accusers arrive." Then he ordered Paul to be guarded in Herod's palace, namely the Praetorium.
